target_description.data: The microRNA miR-33a (hsa-miR-33a-5p, MI0000735, Human) is located within the SREBF2 gene on chromosome 17p11.2 (pri-miR-33a-2), sharing its biogenesis with the host gene that encodes the sterol regulatory element-binding protein 2. The pre-miRNA is characterized by a 70-nucleotide hairpin structure. The dominant functional strand is miR-33a-5p, derived from the 5' arm, while the passenger strand, miR-33a-3p, has limited or unconfirmed activity. miR-33a is most strongly associated with Cardiovascular Disease (CVD), particularly Atherosclerosis, as evidenced by its elevated expression in human atherosclerotic plaques, perturbation studies in mouse models, and correlations with HDL-C levels in circulation. Mechanistically, miR-33a-5p regulates lipid metabolism by targeting genes involved in cholesterol efflux, such as ABCA1 and ABCG1, while promoting fatty acid synthesis through inhibition of AMPKα1. Upstream stimuli include cholesterol depletion and LXR activation, leading to reduced HDL formation and increased lipid accumulation in macrophages, contributing to foam cell formation. Pathophysiologically, by suppressing ABCA1/G1, miR-33a impairs reverse cholesterol transport, accelerating foam cell transformation, a key event in atherosclerotic plaque development. Translationally, miR-33a is being investigated as a therapeutic target for CVD, with anti-miRs (Antagomirs) aimed at enhancing ABCA1 expression and HDL levels, thus linking cholesterol homeostasis to inflammation.
